title: Before You Open a Second Site: The Expansion Readiness Check
subtitle: A second location multiplies your problems before it multiplies your revenue. Are you ready?

toc.label: The Business Plan You Actually Need
Systems That Must Exist Before You Scale

tools: T167-01 · Year in Review Reflection Template

metrics.label: your business plan should fit on one page

sections.heading: The Second-Site Fantasy
sections.body:
Every cafe owner who has built something good thinks about a second location. The logic seems obvious: if one cafe makes money, two will make twice as much.

This is the most dangerous assumption in hospitality.

A second site does not double your revenue. It doubles your complexity. You now need two sets of staff, two supply chains, two sets of equipment maintenance, two rent payments — and one of you. The owner who was present every day, setting the standard, catching problems, greeting regulars — that person now has to choose which cafe to be in. And the one they are not in starts to drift.

The cafes that expand successfully share one trait: they systemised Site 1 before opening Site 2. The coffee is consistent because there are recipe cards and calibration logs, not because the owner is standing at the machine. The service is consistent because there are standards, training, and accountability — not because the owner is watching.

If your first site cannot run well for two weeks without you being there, you are not ready for a second.

sections.heading: The Business Plan You Actually Need
sections.body:
Most cafe business plans are either non-existent or 40-page documents written for a bank that nobody ever reads again. Neither is useful.

Use T164-01 (One-Page Business Summary Template) to create a living document that fits on a single page:

**Mission** — One sentence. Why does this cafe exist? Not "to serve great coffee" — everyone says that. What makes you different? "To be the neighbourhood living room where people feel at home" or "To bring specialty coffee to a high street that only has chains."

**Target Customer** — Who are you for? Be specific. "Working professionals aged 25–45 within a 10-minute walk" is useful. "Everyone" is not.

**Revenue Model** — Where does the money come from? Coffee (what % of revenue), food (what %), retail, catering, delivery? What is the current split and what should it be?

**Key Numbers** — Current monthly revenue, gross margin, labour cost %, rent %, net profit. If you cannot fill these in, that is the first problem to solve.

**3 Priorities This Year** — Not 10. Three things that will make the biggest difference. "Increase food attachment rate to 40%" or "Reduce staff turnover below 30%" or "Launch catering service."

**Risks** — What could go wrong? Key person dependency, lease expiry, supplier price increase, new competitor. Name them so you can plan for them.

Review this page quarterly. If your strategy does not fit on one page, it is not a strategy — it is a wish list.

sections.heading: Market Analysis: Is There Demand?
sections.body:
Before committing to a new location, answer these questions with data, not hope.

Use T165-01 (Competitor Analysis Template):

**Foot traffic.** Sit outside the proposed location at 8am, 12pm, and 4pm on a weekday and a Saturday. Count passersby. Estimate what percentage might convert. A beautiful site on a quiet street may look perfect but have no demand.

**Competition.** Map every cafe, coffee shop, and food-to-go within a 5-minute walk. For each one:
- What do they do well?
- What do they do badly?
- What is their price point?
- How busy are they?
- What is their Google rating?

You need a clear gap to fill. "Better coffee" is not enough of a gap if there are already three specialty cafes nearby. "The only cafe with proper food and workspace" might be.

**Demographics.** Who lives and works within a 10-minute walk? Office workers (weekday demand, morning peak), residents (weekend demand, broader hours), students (price-sensitive, long dwell), tourists (seasonal, higher spend). Your menu and pricing must match the local population.

**Rent economics.** The location may be perfect, but if rent exceeds 15% of projected revenue, the maths are brutal. Calculate: estimated daily revenue × trading days × 12 months = annual revenue estimate. If annual rent is more than 15% of that, negotiate harder or walk away.

**Cannibalisation.** Is the new site close enough to your existing site to steal its customers? Two sites 10 minutes apart may be competing with each other, not growing the market.

sections.heading: The Expansion Readiness Scorecard
sections.body:
Use T166-01 (Expansion Readiness Scorecard) to honestly assess whether your operation can support a second location. Score each area 1–5:

| Area | Question | Score |
|------|----------|-------|
| Financial stability | Has Site 1 been profitable for 6+ consecutive months? | /5 |
| Systems documentation | Are all core processes documented (recipes, SOPs, checklists)? | /5 |
| Team independence | Can Site 1 run for 2 weeks without the owner present? | /5 |
| Management depth | Is there a manager or shift lead ready to run a site? | /5 |
| Training programme | Can you onboard a new barista to standard in 2 weeks? | /5 |
| Supply chain | Are supplier relationships strong enough to support two sites? | /5 |
| Brand clarity | Is the brand identity clear enough to replicate? | /5 |
| Financial reserves | Do you have 6 months of operating costs for the new site in reserve? | /5 |
| Technology | Can your POS, inventory, and scheduling systems support two sites? | /5 |
| Owner capacity | Does the owner have capacity to manage the opening without burning out? | /5 |

**40+ out of 50:** You are likely ready. Proceed with planning.
**30–39:** You have gaps. Address them before committing.
**Below 30:** You are not ready. Expanding now would put both sites at risk.

Be honest. The excitement of a new project can bias the scoring. Ask your manager, your accountant, or a trusted mentor to score it independently and compare.

sections.heading: Systems That Must Exist Before You Scale
sections.body:
Scaling without systems is how good cafes become mediocre chains. Before opening Site 2, these must be in place at Site 1:

**Recipe and quality standards.** Every drink and food item has a written recipe card with photos. Espresso parameters are documented and calibrated daily. Quality checks happen routinely. If you remove yourself from the quality chain and nothing changes, the system works.

**Opening, closing, and shift routines.** Documented checklists for every routine task. The morning does not depend on one person knowing what to do — it depends on a checklist that anyone can follow.

**Onboarding programme.** A structured 5-shift programme that takes a new hire from day one to competence. You will need to hire an entire team for Site 2 — you cannot train them all personally.

**Financial reporting.** Weekly reviews of revenue, cost of goods, labour cost, and margin. If you are not doing this for one site, you will be flying blind across two.

**Inventory management.** Par levels, ordering systems, stock counts. Two sites means two sets of orders, two deliveries, two potential stock-outs.

**Communication rhythm.** Team meetings, shift handovers, and a way to communicate across sites. What works face-to-face in one small cafe breaks down when you are in a different building.

The test is simple: if you disappeared for a month, would Site 1 maintain its standards? If the answer is no, the system is not ready to be replicated.

sections.heading: The Annual Strategic Review
sections.body:
Whether you expand or not, every cafe needs an annual strategy review. This is the moment to zoom out from daily operations and ask: where is this business going?

Use T167-01 (Year in Review Reflection Template):

**What went well this year?**
Name three concrete wins. Revenue growth, a successful new menu launch, a team member who developed into a leader, a Google rating that climbed. Recognise progress — it fuels motivation.

**What did not go well?**
Name three honest disappointments. A staff member who left, a month where you lost money, a food safety near-miss, a competitor who opened nearby. No blame — just clarity.

**What did you learn?**
The most valuable question. "We learned that Saturday brunch is our most profitable service" or "We learned that hiring for attitude and training for skill works better than the reverse."

**What are the 3 priorities for next year?**
Not 10. Three. Specific, measurable, achievable. "Increase ATV from €4.50 to €5.20" or "Launch a catering service generating €1,000/month by Q3" or "Reduce staff turnover to below 25%."

**What is the 3-year vision?**
Where do you want to be? Second site, lifestyle business, franchise, sale? There is no wrong answer — but there needs to be an answer, because it shapes every decision you make this year.

Block half a day for this review. Do it off-site if possible — a change of environment helps strategic thinking. Bring data, not just feelings. The review should be informed by your quarterly business reviews and POS data, not by impressions.

The cafe that reviews its strategy annually and acts on three priorities will outperform the one that works hard every day without looking up.
